questions.q: In the expression $n + 6$, what does the pronumeral $n$ represent?

options.text: A number whose value is not yet known
$6$, the number it is added to
options.why: Reads the constant $6$ as the value of $n$ instead of $n$ standing for its own number.

options.text: The letter $n$, used only as a label
options.why: Treats the pronumeral as a name or label rather than a symbol standing for a number.
Confuses the pronumeral with the addition operation written next to it.

solution.math: A pronumeral stands in for a number that is not yet known.

questions.q: The pronumeral $d$ stands for the number of days in a chosen month, which may be $28$, $30$ or $31$. Which statement is correct?

options.text: $d$ is a variable because it can take several values
$d$ is a constant because a month is one thing
options.why: Fixes on the single word 'month' instead of the several different values $d$ can take.

options.text: $d$ is not a pronumeral because it has many values
options.why: Wrongly assumes a symbol with more than one possible value stops being a pronumeral.

options.text: $d$ is a variable because $d$ comes after $c$
options.why: Bases the classification on the letter's alphabet position rather than on how many values it can take.

solution.math: $d$ can equal $28$, $30$ or $31$ — many values, so it is a variable.

questions.q: In the expression $a + a + b$, the first $a$ equals $4$. What is the value of the second $a$?

options.why: Takes the value from the next term's pronumeral instead of matching the identical letter $a$.
Doubles the value, treating $a + a$ as already combined into $2a$ and evaluated.
Assumes each $a$ is independent, missing that identical pronumerals share one value in an expression.

solution.math: The same pronumeral has the same value, so the second $a$ is also $4$.

questions.q: A cup holds $c$ counters and a separate box holds $b$ counters, where the amounts are unknown and may differ. Which statement is correct?

options.text: $c$ and $b$ may stand for different numbers
$c$ and $b$ must be equal because both count counters
options.why: Assumes different pronumerals must share a value because the objects counted are similar.

options.text: $c$ and $b$ both equal $0$ until they are counted
options.why: Treats an unknown pronumeral as $0$ rather than a number that is simply not yet known.

options.text: $c$ and $b$ are the same number written two ways
options.why: Thinks two different pronumerals always name one identical quantity.

solution.math: Different pronumerals may represent different numbers, so $c$ and $b$ can differ.

questions.q: In 'let $s$ be the number of sides of a square', is $s$ a variable?

options.text: No, because a square always has $4$ sides
Yes, because any letter in maths is a variable
options.why: Assumes every pronumeral is automatically a variable, ignoring that a variable must take many values.

options.text: Yes, because $s$ could be any number of sides
options.why: Overlooks that the context fixes the count at $4$, so $s$ cannot vary.

options.text: No, because $s$ is not the letter $x$ or $y$
options.why: Bases the decision on which letter is used rather than on whether the value can change.

solution.math: A square has exactly $4$ sides, so $s = 4$ is fixed — not a variable.
